var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Four Stars
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Four Stars
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import re

# Load books
with open(locals()['var_function-call-3698234832688086605'], 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

# Load reviews
with open(locals()['var_function-call-4608175155333918877'], 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Function to extract year
def extract_year(text):
    if not text:
        return None
    # Look for year after published/released
    # Case insensitive
    match = re.search(r'(?:published|released).*?\b((?:19|20)\d{2})\b', text, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        return int(match.group(1))
    
    # Fallback: look for specific date format like "January 1, 2004" or "January 2004" anywhere if published keyword not found or match fail
    # But usually published is there.
    # Let's try to match date patterns generally if the first specific one failed
    match_date = re.search(r'\b(?:Jan|Feb|Mar|Apr|May|Jun|Jul|Aug|Sep|Oct|Nov|Dec)[a-z]*\s+(?:\d{1,2},?\s+)?((?:19|20)\d{2})\b', text, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match_date:
        return int(match_date.group(1))
        
    return None

books_df['year'] = books_df['details'].apply(extract_year)

# Drop books without year
books_with_year = books_df.dropna(subset=['year']).copy()
books_with_year['year'] = books_with_year['year'].astype(int)

# Create decade
# 1980s means 1980-1989. Formula: (year // 10) * 10
books_with_year['decade'] = (books_with_year['year'] // 10) * 10
books_with_year['decade_str'] = books_with_year['decade'].astype(str) + 's'

# Join with reviews
# reviews purchase_id matches book_id
merged = pd.merge(reviews_df, books_with_year, left_on='purchase_id', right_on='book_id', how='inner')

# Convert rating to float
merged['rating'] = merged['rating'].astype(float)

# Group by decade
decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_str').agg(
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean'),
    distinct_books=('book_id', 'nunique'),
    num_reviews=('rating', 'count')
).reset_index()

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
filtered_decades = decade_stats[decade_stats['distinct_books'] >= 10]

# Find the one with highest average rating
if not filtered_decades.empty:
    best_decade = filtered_decades.loc[filtered_decades['avg_rating'].idxmax()]
    result = best_decade['decade_str']
    debug_info = filtered_decades.to_dict(orient='records')
else:
    result = "None"
    debug_info = decade_stats.to_dict(orient='records')

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps({"result": result, "debug": debug_info}))

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

import json
import pandas as pd
import re

# Load books
with open(locals()['var_function-call-3698234832688086605'], 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

# Load reviews
with open(locals()['var_function-call-4608175155333918877'], 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Normalize IDs
# Extract number from book_id
books_df['id_num'] = books_df['book_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)
# Extract number from purchase_id
reviews_df['id_num'] = reviews_df['purchase_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)

# Join on id_num
merged = pd.merge(reviews_df, books_df, on='id_num', how='inner')

# Extract year
def extract_year(text):
    if not isinstance(text, str):
        return None
    # Try finding year after "published" or "released"
    match = re.search(r'(?:published|released).*?\b((?:19|20)\d{2})\b', text, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        return int(match.group(1))
    # Fallback to general date pattern
    match_date = re.search(r'\b(?:Jan|Feb|Mar|Apr|May|Jun|Jul|Aug|Sep|Oct|Nov|Dec)[a-z]*\s+(?:\d{1,2},?\s+)?((?:19|20)\d{2})\b', text, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match_date:
        return int(match_date.group(1))
    # Fallback to just 4 digits if it looks like a year (19xx or 20xx)
    # But be careful about ISBNs. Usually ISBNs are 10 or 13 digits.
    # Maybe standalone year?
    # Let's trust the first two methods first.
    return None

merged['year'] = merged['details'].apply(extract_year)

# Drop rows without year
merged = merged.dropna(subset=['year'])
merged['year'] = merged['year'].astype(int)

# Calculate decade
merged['decade'] = (merged['year'] // 10) * 10
# Format as 1980s
merged['decade_str'] = merged['decade'].astype(str) + 's'

# Convert rating to float
merged['rating'] = merged['rating'].astype(float)

# Group by decade
decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_str').agg(
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean'),
    distinct_books=('id_num', 'nunique'),
    count=('rating', 'count')
).reset_index()

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
filtered_decades = decade_stats[decade_stats['distinct_books'] >= 10]

# Sort by avg_rating descending
if not filtered_decades.empty:
    best_decade = filtered_decades.sort_values(by='avg_rating', ascending=False).iloc[0]
    result = best_decade['decade_str']
    debug_info = filtered_decades.to_dict(orient='records')
else:
    result = "None"
    debug_info = decade_stats.to_dict(orient='records')

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps({"result": result, "debug": debug_info}))

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

import json
import pandas as pd
import re

# Load books
with open(locals()['var_function-call-3698234832688086605'], 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

# Load reviews
with open(locals()['var_function-call-4608175155333918877'], 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Normalize IDs
# Extract number from book_id
books_df['id_num'] = books_df['book_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)
# Extract number from purchase_id
reviews_df['id_num'] = reviews_df['purchase_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)

# Join on id_num
merged = pd.merge(reviews_df, books_df, on='id_num', how='inner')

# Extract year
def extract_year(text):
    if not isinstance(text, str):
        return None
    # Try finding year after "published" or "released"
    match = re.search(r'(?:published|released).*?\b((?:19|20)\d{2})\b', text, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        return int(match.group(1))
    # Fallback to general date pattern
    match_date = re.search(r'\b(?:Jan|Feb|Mar|Apr|May|Jun|Jul|Aug|Sep|Oct|Nov|Dec)[a-z]*\s+(?:\d{1,2},?\s+)?((?:19|20)\d{2})\b', text, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match_date:
        return int(match_date.group(1))
    # Fallback to just 4 digits if it looks like a year (19xx or 20xx)
    # But be careful about ISBNs. Usually ISBNs are 10 or 13 digits.
    # Maybe standalone year?
    # Let's trust the first two methods first.
    return None

merged['year'] = merged['details'].apply(extract_year)

# Drop rows without year
merged = merged.dropna(subset=['year'])
merged['year'] = merged['year'].astype(int)

# Calculate decade
merged['decade'] = (merged['year'] // 10) * 10
# Format as 1980s
merged['decade_str'] = merged['decade'].astype(str) + 's'

# Convert rating to float
merged['rating'] = merged['rating'].astype(float)

# Group by decade
decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_str').agg(
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean'),
    distinct_books=('id_num', 'nunique'),
    count=('rating', 'count')
).reset_index()

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
filtered_decades = decade_stats[decade_stats['distinct_books'] >= 10]

# Sort by avg_rating descending
if not filtered_decades.empty:
    best_decade = filtered_decades.sort_values(by='avg_rating', ascending=False).iloc[0]
    result = best_decade['decade_str']
    debug_info = filtered_decades.to_dict(orient='records')
else:
    result = "None"
    debug_info = decade_stats.to_dict(orient='records')

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps({"result": result, "debug": debug_info}))

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

import json
import pandas as pd
import re

# Load books
with open(locals()['var_function-call-3698234832688086605'], 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

# Load reviews

    reviews_data = json.load(f)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Normalize IDs
books_df['id_num'] = books_df['book_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)
reviews_df['id_num'] = reviews_df['purchase_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)

merged = pd.merge(reviews_df, books_df, on='id_num', how='inner')

print("Merged rows:", len(merged))

# Check some details
print("Sample details:", merged['details'].head(5).tolist())

def extract_year_robust(text):
    if not isinstance(text, str):
        return None
    # Try finding year after "published" or "released"
    match = re.search(r'(?:published|released).*?\b((?:19|20)\d{2})\b', text, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        return int(match.group(1))

    match_date = re.search(r'\b(?:Jan|Feb|Mar|Apr|May|Jun|Jul|Aug|Sep|Oct|Nov|Dec)[a-z]*\s+(?:\d{1,2},?\s+)?((?:19|20)\d{2})\b', text, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match_date:
        return int(match_date.group(1))

    # Try finding year in general context, avoiding ISBNs
    # ISBNs usually have hyphens or start with 978...
    # Exclude if preceded by "ISBN" or "ISBN-10" or "ISBN-13" within some distance
    # But simpler: find all 4-digit numbers starting with 19 or 20.
    candidates = re.findall(r'\b((?:19|20)\d{2})\b', text)
    # Filter candidates that look like part of ISBN?
    # ISBN-10: 10 digits. ISBN-13: 13 digits.
    # 4 digits is not an ISBN.
    # But could be page count (e.g. 1996 pages? unlikely high).
    # Could be dimensions?
    # Let's take the first candidate that is not part of a larger number sequence.
    # `\b` handles word boundaries.
    if candidates:
        return int(candidates[0])
    
    return None

print("Rows with year:", merged['year'].notna().sum())
print("Years distribution:", merged['year'].value_counts().head())

merged = merged.dropna(subset=['year'])
merged['year'] = merged['year'].astype(int)
merged['decade'] = (merged['year'] // 10) * 10
merged['decade_str'] = merged['decade'].astype(str) + 's'
merged['rating'] = merged['rating'].astype(float)

decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_str').agg(
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean'),
    distinct_books=('id_num', 'nunique'),
    count=('rating', 'count')
).reset_index()

print("Decade stats:", decade_stats.to_dict(orient='records'))

filtered_decades = decade_stats[decade_stats['distinct_books'] >= 10]

if not filtered_decades.empty:
    best_decade = filtered_decades.sort_values(by='avg_rating', ascending=False).iloc[0]
    result = best_decade['decade_str']

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Four Stars
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import re

# Load books
with open(locals()['var_function-call-3698234832688086605'], 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

# Load reviews
with open(locals()['var_function-call-4608175155333918877'], 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Normalize IDs
# Extract number from book_id
books_df['id_num_book'] = books_df['book_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)
# Extract number from purchase_id
reviews_df['id_num_review'] = reviews_df['purchase_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)

# Check overlap
common_ids = set(books_df['id_num_book']).intersection(set(reviews_df['id_num_review']))
print("Common IDs count:", len(common_ids))
print("Sample common IDs:", list(common_ids)[:5])

# Print some IDs from each
print("Books IDs sample:", books_df['id_num_book'].head().tolist())
print("Reviews IDs sample:", reviews_df['id_num_review'].head().tolist())

if common_ids:
    merged = pd.merge(reviews_df, books_df, left_on='id_num_review', right_on='id_num_book', how='inner')
    print("Merged rows:", len(merged))
else:
    print("No overlap found.")

import json
import pandas as pd
import re

# Load books
with open(locals()['var_function-call-3698234832688086605'], 'r') as f:

with open(locals()['var_function-call-4608175155333918877'], 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Normalize IDs
books_df['id_num_book'] = books_df['book_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)
reviews_df['id_num_review'] = reviews_df['purchase_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)

# Check overlap
common_ids = set(books_df['id_num_book']).intersection(set(reviews_df['id_num_review']))

    "books_ids_sample": books_df['id_num_book'].head().tolist(),

}

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Four Stars
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import re

# Load books
with open(locals()['var_function-call-3698234832688086605'], 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

# Normalize IDs
books_df['id_num'] = books_df['book_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)

def extract_year(text):
    if not isinstance(text, str):
        return None
    # Method 1: Pattern "published ... 19xx/20xx"
    match = re.search(r'(?:published|released).*?\b((?:19|20)\d{2})\b', text, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        return int(match.group(1))
    
    # Method 2: Date pattern "Month Day, Year" or "Month Year"
    match_date = re.search(r'\b(?:Jan|Feb|Mar|Apr|May|Jun|Jul|Aug|Sep|Oct|Nov|Dec)[a-z]*\s+(?:\d{1,2},?\s+)?((?:19|20)\d{2})\b', text, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match_date:
        return int(match_date.group(1))

    # Method 3: Any 4 digit number that looks like a year?
    # Exclude if part of ISBN or other clearly non-year contexts?
    # But let's just see what matches method 1 & 2 first.
    return None

books_df['year'] = books_df['details'].apply(extract_year)

# Check results
sample_extraction = books_df[['book_id', 'details', 'year']].head(20).to_dict(orient='records')
year_count = books_df['year'].notna().sum()

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps({"sample": sample_extraction, "count": int(year_count)}))

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Four Stars
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

# Test regex 2

import json
import pandas as pd
import re

# Load books
with open(locals()['var_function-call-3698234832688086605'], 'r') as f:

# Test regex 2

m2_res = match2

# Test regex 3 with DOTALL
match3 = re.search(r'(?:published|released).*?\b((?:19|20)\d{2})\b', sample_text, re.IGNORECASE | re.DOTALL)

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Four Stars
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import re

# Load books
with open(locals()['var_function-call-3698234832688086605'], 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

# Load reviews
with open(locals()['var_function-call-4608175155333918877'], 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Normalize IDs
books_df['id_num'] = books_df['book_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)
reviews_df['id_num'] = reviews_df['purchase_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)

# Join
merged = pd.merge(reviews_df, books_df, on='id_num', how='inner')

# Extract year using findall
def extract_year_findall(text):
    if not isinstance(text, str):
        return None
    # Find all 4-digit numbers starting with 19 or 20
    matches = re.findall(r'\b((?:19|20)\d{2})\b', text)
    if matches:
        return int(matches[0])
    return None

merged['year'] = merged['details'].apply(extract_year_findall)

# Filter valid years
merged = merged.dropna(subset=['year'])
merged['year'] = merged['year'].astype(int)

# Decade
merged['decade'] = (merged['year'] // 10) * 10
merged['decade_str'] = merged['decade'].astype(str) + 's'

# Convert rating
merged['rating'] = merged['rating'].astype(float)

# Aggregate
decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_str').agg(
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean'),
    distinct_books=('id_num', 'nunique'),
    count=('rating', 'count')
).reset_index()

# Filter >= 10 distinct books
valid_decades = decade_stats[decade_stats['distinct_books'] >= 10]

# Find best decade
if not valid_decades.empty:
    best_decade = valid_decades.sort_values(by='avg_rating', ascending=False).iloc[0]
    result = best_decade['decade_str']
    debug = valid_decades.to_dict(orient='records')
else:
    result = "None"
    debug = decade_stats.to_dict(orient='records')

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps({"result": result, "debug": debug}))

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Four Stars
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import re

# Load books
with open(locals()['var_function-call-3698234832688086605'], 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

# Load reviews
with open(locals()['var_function-call-4608175155333918877'], 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Normalize IDs
books_df['id_num'] = books_df['book_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)
reviews_df['id_num'] = reviews_df['purchase_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)

# Join
merged = pd.merge(reviews_df, books_df, on='id_num', how='inner')

# Extract year
def extract_year_findall(text):
    if not isinstance(text, str):
        return None
    matches = re.findall(r'\b((?:19|20)\d{2})\b', text)
    if matches:
        return int(matches[0])
    return None

merged['year'] = merged['details'].apply(extract_year_findall)

# Check counts
merged_count = len(merged)
year_count = merged['year'].notna().sum()
sample_year = merged['year'].head().tolist() if year_count > 0 else []

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps({"merged_count": merged_count, "year_count": int(year_count), "sample_year": sample_year}))

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Four Stars
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import re

# Load books
with open(locals()['var_function-call-3698234832688086605'], 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

# Load reviews
with open(locals()['var_function-call-4608175155333918877'], 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Normalize IDs
books_df['id_num'] = books_df['book_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)
reviews_df['id_num'] = reviews_df['purchase_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)

# Join
merged = pd.merge(reviews_df, books_df, on='id_num', how='inner')

# Inspect first row details
first_details = merged.iloc[0]['details']
details_type = str(type(first_details))
matches = re.findall(r'\b((?:19|20)\d{2})\b', str(first_details))

debug_info = {
    "details": first_details,
    "type": details_type,
    "matches": matches
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(debug_info))

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Four Stars
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
import re

# Load books
with open(locals()['var_function-call-3698234832688086605'], 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

# Load reviews
with open(locals()['var_function-call-4608175155333918877'], 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Normalize IDs
books_df['id_num'] = books_df['book_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)
reviews_df['id_num'] = reviews_df['purchase_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)

# Join
merged = pd.merge(reviews_df, books_df, on='id_num', how='inner')

# Inspect first row details
first_details = merged.iloc[0]['details']

matches_nob = re.findall(r'((?:19|20)\d{2})', str(first_details))
matches_b = re.findall(r'\b((?:19|20)\d{2})\b', str(first_details))

debug_info = {
    "details": first_details,
    "matches_nob": matches_nob,
    "matches_b": matches_b
}

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(debug_info))

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Four Stars
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!

import json
import pandas as pd
import re

# Load books
with open(locals()['var_function-call-3698234832688086605'], 'r') as f:
    books_data = json.load(f)
books_df = pd.DataFrame(books_data)

# Load reviews
with open(locals()['var_function-call-4608175155333918877'], 'r') as f:
    reviews_data = json.load(f)
reviews_df = pd.DataFrame(reviews_data)

# Normalize IDs
books_df['id_num'] = books_df['book_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)
reviews_df['id_num'] = reviews_df['purchase_id'].str.extract(r'(\d+)').astype(int)

# Join
merged = pd.merge(reviews_df, books_df, on='id_num', how='inner')

# Extract year
def extract_year(text):
    if not isinstance(text, str):
        return None
    # Use lookaround to ensure 4 digits are not part of longer number
    # and match 19xx or 20xx
    matches = re.findall(r'(?<!\d)((?:19|20)\d{2})(?!\d)', text)
    if matches:
        return int(matches[0])
    return None

merged['year'] = merged['details'].apply(extract_year)

# Filter valid years
merged = merged.dropna(subset=['year'])
merged['year'] = merged['year'].astype(int)

# Decade
merged['decade'] = (merged['year'] // 10) * 10
merged['decade_str'] = merged['decade'].astype(str) + 's'

# Convert rating
merged['rating'] = merged['rating'].astype(float)

# Aggregate
decade_stats = merged.groupby('decade_str').agg(
    avg_rating=('rating', 'mean'),
    distinct_books=('id_num', 'nunique'),
    count=('rating', 'count')
).reset_index()

# Filter decades with at least 10 distinct books
filtered_decades = decade_stats[decade_stats['distinct_books'] >= 10]

# Sort by avg_rating descending
if not filtered_decades.empty:
    best_decade = filtered_decades.sort_values(by='avg_rating', ascending=False).iloc[0]
    result = best_decade['decade_str']
    avg_rating = best_decade['avg_rating']
    debug_info = filtered_decades.to_dict(orient='records')
else:
    result = "None"
    avg_rating = 0.0
    debug_info = decade_stats.to_dict(orient='records')

print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps({"result": result, "avg_rating": avg_rating, "debug": debug_info}))

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: Ha! On me!  I thought this was a cookbook!
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are "Tasting Notes" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text: Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.

var_function-call-10897355955636571403.title: A wonderful adventure in France
var_function-call-10897355955636571403.text:
I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!
